creator: marklmi26-fbsd@yahoo.com
text:
I was not directly trying to build db5. It just showed up via dependencies. I'm not familiar with db5 at all (usage or its code). I just recorded a form of what the compiler reported.



The little looking around I've done suggests that the whole code base or large parts of it is old K&R C code instead of "ANSI C" (or its later variants): no use of function prototypes that I saw in the little that I looked at.

Converting to modern C would look to be a large effort if that generalizes to the whole code base.

The old syntax does not implicitly convert a call's arguments to the types of the parameters declared in the routine called. In fact the routine being called need not even be declared when called (if the old default return type is appropriate). The call can establish a stack layout that is not what the called routine expects, no matter if the called routine was pre-declared or not.



The most obvious thing to change for the specific reports that the compiler made would be to use:

                                onpd_sz += item_space(dbp, h,
                                                      (db_indx_t) (indx + O_INDX));
. . .
                                data_sz = item_size(dbp, h,
                                                    (db_indx_t) (indx + O_INDX));

In other words: casting so that the call arguments match the routines called for the types of the parameters with the compiler-reported problem, forcing the stack layout to match.

I've no clue if the db5 code is careful to avoid indx + O_INDX overflowing db_indx_t. This coding would just silently truncate the value for such an overflow.



As for without any changes. . .

The indx parameter in both item_space and item_size will have garbage values for big-endian machines (powerpc family, sparc family) and for pdp-endian families (if any) for the calls that use "indx + O_INDX". [I've no clue if FreeBSD supports anything but big endian and little endian memory layouts for integers.] For big-endian 32 bit int contexts indx would access just the high half of the 32 bit int value that was put on the stack (that half of the value would likely normally or always be zero). The half that was missed/ignored would have the actual value intended --unless the overall value overflowed the 16 bits.

Such is the way old K&R C worked and the way the old syntax for function headers still works when the syntax is allowed and in use.

Garbage-in/garbage-out for such contexts when the call and called do not match, such as for big-endian contexts. I do not plan to put effort analyzing the db5 code base to find any of the other specific forms of garbage-out that result as a consequence. (Sorry.) It is enough for me to know that for some FreeBSD environments the wrong 16 bit value will be used: that is enough garbage/"practical impact" for me.

I sometimes use powerpc's [a big-endian context] under FreeBSD but it will be weeks/months before I again have access. Otherwise the live systems that I currently access to are little endian. [Little endian hides the issue by the memory layout happening to access the 16 bit half with the desired bits in it: the low half.] So I can not demonstrate/test big-endian issues with a live big-endian system currently.

[I've never set up a virtual machine emulation of a different-than-live TARGET_ARCH. As I understand this is technically possible. That would be one way to explore db5 operation for big endian contexts.]

Also: As I've no db5 knowledge I've no clue what appropriate db5 tests would be like for a big-endian system.
